transcriptChunks: Today we are presenting our final ENES 104 group reflection. Our group members are Antranig Tatarian, Joshua Fawole, and Natneal Belete.
Our goal is to explain the main lessons we took from the course speakers and how those lessons changed the way we think about engineering, professionalism, and teamwork.
Across the semester, a few themes stood out to us as a group: professionalism, problem-solving, team collaboration, perseverance in industry, and the pursuit of knowledge.
These ideas kept showing up in different speakers' stories, even when they came from different industries or career paths.
For us, the biggest point was that success in engineering is not only about technical ability. It also depends on how you communicate, adapt, learn, and contribute to a team.
For my personal reflection, the speaker who stood out most was Dr. Joseph Varghese because he gave a very real perspective on working in Silicon Valley and in industry overall.

One idea I took from him was the importance of interviewing the interviewer. Instead of only trying to impress a company, you also need to understand whether that environment is the right place for you to grow.

Another lesson was that industry often works with a 'good enough' standard. That does not mean doing poor work; it means knowing when something meets the real requirement instead of chasing perfection forever.

Dr. Varghese also emphasized that what you know today can become outdated quickly. That made me think differently about engineering because continuous learning is not optional; it is part of the career.

From Dr. Islam, I took away the importance of being reliable and being comfortable saying 'I do not know.' In a team or startup environment, being honest and willing to learn can matter more than pretending to have every answer.
Joshua's reflection also connected strongly to the idea of impact. From Dr. Joseph Varghese, we learned that simply listing participation or accomplishments on a resume is not enough.
The stronger approach is to ask what changed because of your work. If you can show numerical, personal, or professional impact, then your experience becomes more meaningful.

From Dr. Daniel Juliano, Joshua took away the importance of maximizing every opportunity. Some early opportunities may not look impressive at first, but they can still build skills, confidence, and professional growth.

Dr. Rashid Islam's hovercraft example showed that useful knowledge can come from people with different backgrounds. That reminded us that teamwork works best when people stay open to ideas outside their own specialty.

Dr. Faranak Nekoogar's example also showed the importance of solving practical problems. Her RFID idea came from listening to a real issue firefighters faced, and that solution later became useful in shipping and tracking systems.
For CIP-1, one thing that worked in ENES 104 was hearing from professionals with different experiences. It made the course feel more connected to real industry instead of only classroom concepts.

One thing that could be improved is setting clearer professionalism expectations earlier in the semester. If students understood the expected structure and behavior from the start, the class could feel more organized.
For example, the first few sessions could include clearer expectations for participation, speaker questions, and how students should connect the speaker lessons to their own goals.
For CIP-2, we used the team-building workshop mainly to understand how each person preferred to contribute. That helped us divide the reflection work based on what each person connected with most.

For feedback, we compared our main takeaways and discussed which points overlapped. Then we combined the strongest themes into one group message instead of keeping three completely separate reflections.
One thing we could improve is making the group presentation more balanced. Some slides have more detail than others, so the final version should make each person's section feel equally developed.
We could also improve the visual design by cutting down text and using the slides more as speaking guides rather than full notes.
Overall, ENES 104 helped us see that engineering success depends on technical skill, but also on professionalism, adaptability, impact, and the ability to keep learning from every opportunity.

facultyAINotes.reason: This is a good final reflection moment and would test whether the conclusion is specific enough.
